author: maintainer-agent

patch.value:
pm context and pm next currently assemble their working set from structural rules (dependency-aware actionability per ADR pm-9x6x: ready = active leaf with no open blocked_by, ordered by compareCriticalItems). That is correct but shallow: it uses only a fraction of the data the tracker already records, and it does not adapt between a 10-item hobby workspace and a million-item, decades-spanning one.

Proposal: a pluggable relevance-scoring engine (SDK-first) that ranks candidate items for context assembly by combining ALL available signals:
- recency and activity density from the per-item history streams (recently touched, recently discussed, aging-WIP)
- dependency-graph proximity to the agent's focus item, active claims, and current plan steps
- structural priority signals: priority, risk, severity, deadline pressure, sprint/release membership
- semantic similarity between the agent's focus/query and item corpus (reusing the existing hybrid search providers)
- knowledge density: items carrying learnings/decisions relevant to the working set get surfaced so past lessons are re-injected instead of re-learned
- author affinity: what this agent (PM_AUTHOR) touched before

Design constraints:
- Scoring lives in the SDK as a documented, replaceable interface (scoreContextCandidates); the built-in scorer is just the default implementation — projects can ship their own via extensions.
- Deterministic and explainable: --explain-ranking (or a score breakdown in json output) shows per-signal contributions, so agents and humans can audit why an item was surfaced.
- Token-budgeted assembly: given a budget (explicit flag or config default), the engine packs the highest-value items and degrades gracefully (tighter projections before dropping items).
- O(result) at scale: signal inputs must come from the derived read index (pm-801d) and search index, never a full workspace scan; benchmark on the pm-mi2x synthetic workspaces.
- Small-workspace neutrality: on tiny workspaces the ranking must reduce to today's sensible ordering with zero configuration.

This is the core of the 'project management = context management' philosophy: the optimal context at any time, computed from all data, metadata, and history the workspace already has.
Adaptive context relevance engine: multi-signal scoring over metadata, graph, and history drives context/next assembly under an explicit token budget

SDK exposes a documented pluggable context-scoring interface with the built-in multi-signal scorer as default; pm context/pm next accept a token-budget bound and pack results by score with graceful projection degradation; json output can include a per-signal score breakdown; ranking is deterministic for a fixed workspace state; hot path stays index-backed (no full scan) and meets pm-9rxu latency targets on the 100k and 1M benchmark workspaces; on small workspaces default ordering remains stable (golden tests); extension can replace the scorer end-to-end (exemplar test)

message: Long-horizon context-quality planning: multi-signal relevance gap

author: codex-agent
message: Split adaptive context relevance engine into implementation slices

author: codex-agent

patch.value: SDK-first adaptive ranking engine for pm context/next: derive multi-signal relevance from tracker metadata, graph, history, semantic search, and agent state, then assemble bounded-token context with explainable scores.

message: Backfill concise description after splitting context engine into child slices

value.author: codex-agent
value.text: Planning evidence 2026-07-07: all-status duplicate checks showed pm-4k6b is the canonical adaptive context engine parent; no focused child existed for signal extraction, scorer contract, or token-budget packing. Added non-duplicate implementation slices: pm-3hps (derived signal feature store), pm-h3no (pluggable SDK scorer contract), and pm-55ra (diversity-aware token-budget packer). Algorithm sketch: build an index-backed candidate set from focus/claim/query/dependency windows; compute normalized signal vectors for recency, activity density, graph proximity, priority/risk/deadline pressure, semantic similarity, knowledge density, author affinity, and continuity deltas; combine them through a deterministic default scorer with extension override; pack by marginal utility per token while preserving required anchors and degrading projection depth before dropping items; expose score breakdowns for explainability and pm-atfm evaluation.

author: codex-agent
message: Adaptive context engine planning slices recorded and verified via pm-doxj linked tests

value.author: maintainer-agent
value.text:
Algorithm brainstorm 2026-07-07 (planning pass) — concrete candidate algorithms for the scorer, extending the body's signal list with the combination machinery:
(1) Scale-free recency via ACT-R base-level activation: activation(i) = ln(sum over accesses t_j of t_j^-d), d~0.5, computed from the item's history stream timestamps. Log-time decay is the one law that behaves identically for a minutes-old scratch workspace and a decades-old archive (the spectrum invariant pm-z436/pm-g2zz demand) and it rewards frequency AND recency in one term, replacing ad-hoc half-life constants.
(2) Spreading activation for associative recall: seed activation at the focus item, active claims, and current plan step; propagate over typed edges (blocked_by/parent/related plus semantic k-NN edges from the hybrid index) with per-edge-type damping; k iterations of sparse matrix-vector products over the derived read index keeps it O(neighborhood), never O(workspace).
(3) Learned signal weights with explainability preserved: keep the final score a LINEAR combination of the per-signal contributions (so --explain-ranking stays truthful), but fit the weights per workspace offline against the pm-atfm eval harness as the objective (pairwise learning-to-rank on 'agent actually used this item next' labels derivable from history streams). Weights persist in settings; default weights ship for cold start.
(4) Token-budget packing as submodular selection: greedy by marginal-value-per-token (MMR-style redundancy penalty so two near-duplicate items do not both spend budget), with per-item projection degradation levels (full body -> summary -> id+title) as fractional knapsack steps. Greedy is provably near-optimal for submodular objectives and is deterministic.
All four stay SDK-side behind scoreContextCandidates; none require new stored state beyond what history streams and the read index already hold.

value.author: maintainer-agent
value.text:
Algorithm brainstorm 2026-07-07 (planning pass, continuation of (1)-(4)):
(5) Reciprocal rank fusion as the cold-start combiner: before a workspace has enough history for (3)'s learned linear weights, combining per-signal RANKS instead of scores — score(i) = sum over signals s of 1/(k + rank_s(i)), k~60 — needs no calibration between signals with incompatible scales (activation vs cosine similarity vs priority ordinals), is deterministic, and is robust to any one signal degenerating (a broken/empty signal ranks everything equally and cancels out). Graduation path: ship RRF as the zero-config default, fit (3)'s linear weights per workspace once pm-atfm labels exist, keep RRF as the documented fallback. Explainability holds: per-signal rank contributions are exactly reportable in --explain-ranking.
(6) Write-time incremental signal maintenance: at millions of items (pm-g2zz) even O(candidates) re-scoring at read time is dominated by recomputing per-item activation/affinity from raw history streams. Because every mutation already lands one immutable history entry, fold signal state incrementally at write time: each history append updates that item's running activation term, author-affinity counters, and semantic-neighborhood invalidation in the derived read index (pm-801d) — an event-sourced materialized view of the scorer inputs. Read-time work drops to combine-and-pack (4); rebuild-from-history stays the recovery path, preserving the derived-never-authoritative invariant. This is also the natural home for the per-author watermarks that pm-vvib candidates #4 novelty and #5 theory-of-mind derive — one fold pass maintains all of them.

value.author: maintainer-agent
value.text:
Algorithm brainstorm 2026-07-07 (planning pass, continuation of (1)-(6)):
(7) Two-stage retrieval funnel with per-source candidate quotas — (1)-(6) specify how to SCORE and PACK, but at millions of items (pm-g2zz) the scorer must never see the whole workspace; the missing explicit piece is the recall stage of the classic recall-then-rank architecture. Candidate generation unions bounded top-k slates from independent cheap sources, each with its own quota: (a) ANN top-k from the hybrid semantic index, (b) n-hop graph expansion from focus/claims/plan steps (the seed set of (2)), (c) top-k by folded activation from the write-time view of (6) (a simple sorted structure makes this O(k)), (d) the agent's own WIP/claimed set and watermark-delta (novelty candidates), (e) exact lexical/field-predicate hits. Per-source quotas guarantee slate DIVERSITY structurally (no single degenerate signal floods the pool) and make cost independent of workspace size: scoring is O(sum of quotas), never O(items). Quotas scale with the token budget, which is how the same funnel serves a 3-item scratch workspace (every source returns everything, union is the whole workspace) and a decades-deep archive unchanged — the spectrum invariant. Explainability extends naturally: --explain-ranking reports which source(s) recalled each candidate before the per-signal score breakdown. SDK-side behind scoreContextCandidates like (1)-(6); composes directly with (5) RRF, which can fuse the per-source ranks as its cold-start combiner.
